findings.title: Between-turn permission waits can be evicted as "idle"
findings.description: The new permission flow now blocks for a Discord decision in code paths that are outside the primary user turn. In these loops the session is waiting on `resp_rx`, but `is_turn_active` is not set and `last_activity` is not refreshed when the permission request is created. TTL/LRU eviction only exempts sessions with `is_turn_active` or active background tasks, so a session that is merely waiting on a permission button can be swept as idle and the waiter will see `Err(_)`/"session terminated". This is a real behavior change from the previous synchronous deny path for between-turn/background requests.
findings.suggestion: Track permission waits as active session state. Either set `is_turn_active` (or add a dedicated `is_waiting_for_permission` flag) for the whole permission sub-loop, and refresh `last_activity` when the request is issued and when activity arrives in every permission-wait branch.

findings.title: Interrupted or aborted permission requests leave stale pending entries and buttons
findings.description: Once `run_permission_handler` has inserted a `PendingPermission`, several new branches resolve the request without touching that map entry: interrupt branches write a deny response directly to stdin, and EOF/error/session-termination paths just break out. The stored `oneshot::Sender` then remains in `pending_permissions` until a later full-session cleanup or until someone clicks the stale Discord button. On a still-running session this leaks pending entries and exposes buttons for requests that have already been denied or abandoned.
findings.suggestion: Centralize permission finalization so every exit path removes the `request_id` from `pending_permissions` and updates/disables the Discord message when the request is cancelled, interrupted, or the session exits.

findings.title: Session teardown no longer owns permission cleanup
findings.description: With the receiver moved into a dedicated permission task, pending-permission cleanup is now scattered across callers (`message.rs`, command handlers, TTL loop) instead of being part of `SessionManager` teardown. The current diff patched the known call sites, but the invariant is now easy to miss: any future direct `kill_session`/eviction path that forgets the extra `pending_permissions.retain(...)` will leak senders and leave dead Discord components behind.
findings.suggestion: Move pending-permission cleanup into `SessionManager` itself, or add a single teardown helper that aborts the worker, aborts the permission handler, kills the child, and removes all pending permissions for that thread.

summary: The refactor improves ownership of the permission receiver, but it introduces lifecycle gaps around permission waits and cleanup that can terminate live requests or leave stale pending state behind.
